question_title: What signed value, in billions of U.S. dollars, will the U.S. Census Bureau first report for the March 2026 advance international trade balance in goods?
summary:
The March 2026 advance goods trade balance will be the first advance economic indicator report after the January and February suspensions. The forecast centers on -92.1B (median), reflecting:

1. Post-tariff regime baseline: The May 2025-Jan 2026 period averaged -85.7B with high volatility (std 12.5B).
2. Tariff reduction impact: The Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs on Feb 20, 2026, and the replacement Section 122 tariff is lower (ETR from 10.3% to 7.7%). Lower tariffs should boost imports and widen the deficit by roughly $5-10B.
3. KPMG explicitly expects more imports in Feb/March, calling the recent narrowing "short-lived."
4. Offsetting factor: NRF port data shows import container volumes still declining YoY.

The 80% CI spans [-108.7, -77.6], reflecting the extreme monthly volatility observed in this series during the post-tariff era (individual months ranged from -58.6B to -99.2B in the recent regime).

factors.description: Post-tariff regime (May 2025-Jan 2026) has mean of -85.7B with std 12.5B, establishing the baseline range for the current trade environment.

IEEPA tariffs struck down Feb 20, 2026 and replaced with lower Section 122 (10%). Effective tariff rate drops from 10.3% to 7.7% (Penn Wharton). Lower tariffs should boost imports and widen the deficit by roughly $5-10B starting in March.

KPMG analysis (March 2026) explicitly states the trade deficit narrowing 'could be short-lived' and expects more imports and inventories in Feb/March. High-frequency port data shows increased activity.

AI data center buildout is a strong tailwind for capital goods imports. From the advance report Table 1, Dec 2025 capital goods imports were $107.3B, up 28.1% YoY from $83.8B in Dec 2024. This structural trend continues.

NRF Global Port Tracker (March 2026) says import volumes expected to remain below 2025 levels in H1 2026. Container traffic showed slower start to 2026. This partially offsets the tariff-reduction boost.
Historical March vs January seasonal pattern: March is typically 0-3B wider (more negative) than January. Jan 2026 was -80.8B Census SA, suggesting seasonal norm of ~-83B before other adjustments.
Advance reports for Jan and Feb were suspended; March will be the first since December. Potential for unusual coverage or processing effects, adding measurement uncertainty.

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary: March 2026 Advance Goods Trade Balance**

| Percentile | Value ($B) |
|-----------|-----------|
| P10 | -108.7 |
| P25 | -100.3 |
| P50 (median) | **-92.1** |
| P75 | -84.4 |
| P90 | -77.6 |

**Key drivers of the forecast:**
- **Baseline:** The post-tariff regime (May 2025-Jan 2026) averaged -85.7B
- **Tariff shift:** IEEPA tariffs struck down Feb 20; replacement Section 122 lowers effective tariff rate from 10.3% to 7.7%, which should widen the deficit
- **Expert analysis:** KPMG explicitly expects the deficit narrowing to be "short-lived" with more imports in Feb/March
- **Offsetting:** Container volumes still declining YoY per NRF port tracker

The advance report is scheduled for **April 29, 2026** and will be the first advance report since the December 2025 report (January and February were suspended).

[Narrowing of the trade deficit could be short-lived](https://kpmg.com/us/en/articles/2026/january-2026-international-trade.html)

[Effective Tariff Rates and Revenues (Updated March 16, 2026) | Penn Wharton Budget Model](https://budgetmodel.wharton.upenn.edu/p/2026-03-16-effective-tariff-rates-and-revenues-updated-march-16-2026/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the numeric value of the U.S. Census Bureau’s first published March 2026 advance international trade balance in goods, in billions of U.S. dollars, using the signed value exactly as published in the Advance Economic Indicators. The primary source is the U.S. Census Bureau Advance Economic Indicators PDF at https://www.census.gov/econ/indicators/advance_report.pdf
fine_print: Use the first March 2026 advance goods balance and ignore later revisions unless the Census Bureau corrects the first release before May 1, 2026. Resolve using the signed number, so a deficit should be recorded as negative if the report presents it that way; round to one decimal place only if the published value is given to greater precision. Candidate Sources: Census foreign-trade schedule https://www.census.gov/foreign-trade/schedule.html ; Census international trade page https://www.census.gov/foreign-trade/data/
